topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box was tucked away in the attic, smelling faintly of old paper and lavender. I hadn't meant to look, but the sheer compulsion to understand the strange, meticulously organized chaos of my mother’s life dragged me up there. Inside, stacked spine-to-spine, were every single assignment from primary school through college. Each piece of paper, neatly labeled with a colored index card, felt like a tiny, damning indictment. My hands trembled as I picked up a geometry proof from sophomore year. It was perfect, flawless, and it belonged to a woman who never seemed to acknowledge my existence. A bitter, tight knot tightened in my chest, and I shoved the box back down, the sound of the lid clicking shut echoing the finality of my disappointment.

[story 2]

I found the collection behind a false panel in the basement, a forgotten treasure trove of paper. As I sifted through the neatly categorized homework, a cold, sharp feeling settled over me. Every neat handwriting, every perfectly executed essay, seemed to mock my own struggles. I ran a finger over a history report, the ink stark against the aged paper, and a wave of profound resentment washed over me. I stood there for a long time, breathing in the musty air, feeling a profound sense of being overlooked, of being utterly insignificant compared to the flawless record my mother had maintained. I dropped the papers, the sound surprisingly loud in the silence, and walked away without another glance.

[story 3]

It was in the spare closet that I stumbled upon it, a cardboard container overflowing with schoolwork. The sheer volume was overwhelming, a monument to a life lived with relentless, unyielding structure. Looking at the sheer quantity of completed work, a surge of intense irritation flared within me. Why? Why did she hoard this evidence of effort? I picked up a stack of literature critiques, my jaw clenching so hard I thought my teeth might crack. The paper felt brittle beneath my touch, and I felt a burning sensation behind my eyes. I didn't want to look closer; the sight of such perfect preservation only amplified the distance between us, leaving me feeling profoundly unsettled and deeply annoyed.
